title: The Geometry of GL-Varieties
speaker: Jan Draisma
abstract: A $\mathrm{GL}$-variety $X$ is an (infinite-dimensional) affine variety with an action of the infinite general linear group $\mathrm{GL}$ such that the coordinate ring of $X$ is a polynomial  $\mathrm{GL}$-representation and generated by finitely many $\mathrm{GL}$-orbits of elements.
tags: Conference on 100 Years of Noetherian Rings

proof_stages.label: Polynomial GL-algebras and finite generation
proof_stages.summary: The talk fixes an algebraically closed field of characteristic zero, defines the infinite general linear group and polynomial GL-modules, and packages compatible commutative algebra objects as finitely generated GL-algebras.

proof_stages.label: Topological GL-varieties and constructibility
proof_stages.summary: Since full equivariant ideal Noetherianity is open, the speaker passes to reduced spectra of finitely generated GL-algebras, states topological GL-Noetherianity, and uses it to obtain Chevalley-type constructibility for equivariant morphisms.

proof_stages.label: Bounded Laurent-curve theorem for closure points
proof_stages.summary: For a GL-equivariant morphism Y to X, any k-point in the closure of the image is asserted to be the t-to-zero limit of the image of a uniformly bounded Laurent-series point of Y.

proof_stages.label: Uniform consequences for polynomial strength
proof_stages.summary: The bounded-curve theorem is applied to strength and border-strength loci of homogeneous polynomials to obtain a bound on negative t-exponents that is uniform in the number of variables.

proof_stages.label: Reduction to ordinary curves in irreducible GL-varieties
proof_stages.summary: The proof is reduced to showing that, in an irreducible GL-variety X, a dense GL-stable open subset U and an irreducible closed GL-subvariety Z can be met by one ordinary affine curve.

proof_stages.label: Shifted local product structure
proof_stages.summary: A shift of the GL-action and localization by a GN-invariant function put an irreducible GL-variety locally in the form B times A_lambda, where curve construction is straightforward away from the possible vanishing on Z.

proof_stages.label: Pair version and codimension-one reduction
proof_stages.summary: To force the curve to hit Z, the local product theorem is strengthened for pairs in codimension one, and a blow-up reduces the general case to that setting.

examples.name: Infinite matrix GL-algebra R0 = Sym(V tensor V)
examples.note: The coordinate ring of countably infinite matrices, with GL acting by congruence, serves as a running concrete model for finitely generated GL-algebras and the open equivariant Noetherianity problem.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker explicitly identifies R0 with an infinite polynomial ring, writes the coordinate variables, computes the GL action as g-transpose times the matrix of variables times g, and later identifies its spectrum/decomposition.

passes.reason: It is the standard infinite polynomial GL-algebra of matrix coordinates, used as a basic test case with unknown Noetherianity rather than as a disproof.

examples.name: Degree-d forms with dense GL-orbits
examples.note: The affine space of degree-d forms on the countable vector space, including the Fermat-type quartic sum, illustrates the pathological abundance of dense GL-orbits in infinite-dimensional affine spaces.
examples.engagement_reason: degree-d forms and examples with dense GL-orbits, including the sum of fourth powers, are described and stated to have dense orbits without a derivation.

passes.reason: These forms have orbit closure equal to the whole degree-d form space, a maximal orbit-closure property, and are used illustratively.

examples.name: Degree-four degeneration showing nonclosed strength locus
examples.note: The Ballico-Bik-Oneto-Ventura degeneration, where negative powers of t cancel in a sum of products, visibly motivates the bounded Laurent-series description of border-strength phenomena.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker sketches the degree-four degeneration by writing a t-dependent product expression, explaining cancellation of negative powers, and identifying the limiting quartic.

passes.reason: It is a boundary degeneration, a limit of strength-at-most-three quartics whose limit has strength four, refuting closedness of the locus.
the degree-four form is a limiting boundary point of the strength-at-most-three locus but has larger actual strength, showing nonclosedness
